en.extract:
The black-naped tern (Sterna sumatrana) is an oceanic tern mostly found in tropical and subtropical areas of the Indian Ocean and western Pacific Ocean. They are rarely found inland. It was described from the islands around Sumatra. It is most closely related to roseate tern S. dougallii and white-fronted tern S. striata.


Description
The black-naped tern is named from the black band beginning at the eyes connecting behind the head and descending the back of the neck. The rest of their plumage is white, with pale silvery-grey wings and mantle, apart from the outermost primary feather which has a narrow black outer web. The tail is white, and deeply forked, extending (like the related roseate and white-fronted terns) beyond the wingtips on perched birds. The beaks and legs are black. They have a body length of 30–35 cm and a wingspan of 66 cm. Adults weigh between 97 and 120 g. Hatchlings are mottled brown.


Taxonomy
There are two listed subspecies:

S. s. sumatrana (Raffles, 1822) – islands of the eastern Indian Ocean through to the western Pacific, north to Taiwan and south to Queensland in Australia
S. s. mathewsi (Stresemann, 1914) – islands of the western Indian Ocean


Distribution and habitat
The black-naped tern inhabits rocky shores throughout the Western-Pacific and Indian oceans, migrating yearly to islets and islands serving as breeding grounds.


Behaviour


Hunting and diet
Like most terns, this species hunts by diving; they partially breach the surface of the water and directly capture their prey carrying it horizontally in their beak. Most hunt alone at sea, but hunt closer to shore in the surf when feeding nestlings and mates.


Diet
The diet consists of small fish up to 10 cm long, including those in families:

Atherinidae (hardyheads and sprats);
Engraulidae (anchovies);
Hemiramphidae (garfish);
Exocoetidae (flying fish);
Mugilidae (mullet);
Sphyroenidae (barracudas);
Carangidae (trevally or jacks);
and Scombridae (tuna).


Reproduction and development


Breeding grounds
The black-naped tern breeds on oceanic islands in small colonies of 5–20 pairs, occasionally up to 200. Nests are made past high tide in depressions in sand or rock, sometimes delineated with shells or coral fragments, otherwise with little preparation. The rocky outcrops may be returned to yearly during the breeding season.


Laying period
Laying time varies from May to December depending on the location of the colony. Egg laying begins in India and Polynesia in May, followed by colonies in the Philippines in July, north-east Australia in September, oceanic Indian colonies from September to November, and the Great Barrier Reef from November to December. Leading up to egg laying, the male feeds the female her daily requirement of fish.


Clutch size and brooding

Clutch size varies from island to island, between 1 and 3 eggs per pair. Both mates take shifts incubating the eggs for 21–23 days; shifts last anywhere between 4 minutes and 7 hours. Once the chicks hatch, the mates alternate in feeding and brooding for the first 7 days. As the chicks reach later stages of development, the mates hunt simultaneously.


Predation
Until the chicks reach a weight of roughly 65 g, they are vulnerable to predation by gulls; at this point, they are too large for the gulls to carry. Development to this weight takes 10.5 days; since the parents protect their nest for the first 7 days, chicks spend 3.5 days more vulnerable to predation. To defend against this, parents camouflage the nest by removing eggshell remnants and defecating several meters away to avoid bright eye-catching material near the chicks. When aerial predators are present, chicks hide themselves among vegetation and debris.
On one occasion, a colony of black-naped terns were observed using mobbing, shrill cries, and defecation as defence mechanisms against an encroaching grey heron during the breeding season.


Fledging
Chicks fledge after 21–23 days; however, fledglings rely on their parents for at least 2 months afterwards. Parents and fledglings therefore leave breeding grounds together.


Calls
This tern calls with short, high-pitched, repeated sharp notes; "chit", "chip", "chrrut", "tsip".


References


External links

Kiru Dhooni

| taxon = Sterna sumatrana
| authority = [[Stamford Raffles|Raffles]], 1822
}}

The '''black-naped tern''' ('''''Sterna sumatrana''''') is an oceanic [[tern]] mostly found in tropical and [[subtropical]] areas of the [[Indian Ocean]] and western [[Pacific Ocean]]. They are rarely found inland.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Smith |first=Geoffrey C. |date=1990 |title=Factors Influencing Egg Laying and Feeding in Black-naped Terns Sterna Sumatrana |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1071/MU9900088 |journal=Emu – Austral Ornithology |language=en |volume=90 |issue=2 |pages=88–96 |doi=10.1071/MU9900088 |bibcode=1990EmuAO..90...88S |issn=0158-4197|url-access=subscription }}</ref> It was described from the islands around [[Sumatra]]. It is most closely related to [[roseate tern]] ''S. dougallii'' and [[white-fronted tern]] ''S. striata''.<ref name="Černý">{{cite journal | last1=Černý | first1=David | last2=Natale | first2=Rossy | title=Comprehensive taxon sampling and vetted fossils help clarify the time tree of shorebirds (Aves, Charadriiformes) | journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution | volume=177 | date=2022 | doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2022.107620 | doi-access=free | article-number=107620 | bibcode=2022MolPE.17707620C | url=https://www.biorxiv.org/content/biorxiv/early/2021/07/16/2021.07.15.452585.full.pdf | access-date=2024-12-22}}</ref>

[[File:Black-naped tern (Sterna sumatrana) adult breeding plumage.jpg|thumb|in breeding plumage]]

==Description==
The black-naped tern is named from the black band beginning at the eyes connecting behind the head and descending the back of the neck. The rest of their plumage is white, with pale silvery-grey wings and mantle, apart from the outermost primary feather which has a narrow black outer web. The tail is white, and deeply forked, extending (like the related roseate and white-fronted terns) beyond the wingtips on perched birds. The beaks and legs are black. They have a body length of 30–35 cm and a wingspan of 66 cm. Adults weigh between 97 and 120 g. Hatchlings are mottled brown.<ref name="Simpson">{{cite book | title=Field guide to the birds of Australia | publisher=Penguin | publication-place=Camberwell, Vic | date=2010 | isbn=978-0-670-07231-6 | page=}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last1=Gochfeld |first1=Michael |last2=Burger |first2=Joanna |last3=Garcia |first3=Ernest |date=2020 |title=Black-naped Tern (Sterna sumatrana), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/blnter1/1.0/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.blnter1.01 |issn=2771-3105|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last1=Deng |first1=S. H. |last2=Lee |first2=T. K. |last3=Wee |first3=Y. C. |date=2008 |title=Black-naped tern (Sterna sumatra Raffles, 1822) mobbing a grey heron (Ardea cinerea Linnaeus,1758) |url=https://lkcnhm.nus.edu.sg/wp-content/uploads/sites/10/app/uploads/2017/06/2008nis117-127.pdf |journal=Nature in Singapore |issue=1 |pages=117–127}}</ref>

== Taxonomy ==
There are two listed [[subspecies]]:<ref name=":1" />
*''S. s. sumatrana'' <small>([[Stamford Raffles|Raffles]], 1822)</small> – islands of the eastern [[Indian Ocean]] through to the western [[Pacific Ocean|Pacific]], north to [[Taiwan]] and south to [[Queensland]] in [[Australia]]
*''S. s. mathewsi'' <small>([[Erwin Stresemann|Stresemann]], 1914)</small> – islands of the western [[Indian Ocean]]

== Distribution and habitat ==
The black-naped tern inhabits rocky shores throughout the [[Western Pacific Ocean|Western-Pacific]] and [[Indian Ocean|Indian]] oceans, [[Bird migration|migrating]] yearly to islets and islands serving as breeding grounds.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Thiebot |first1=Jean-Baptiste |last2=Nakamura |first2=Noboru |last3=Toguchi |first3=Yutaka |last4=Tomita |first4=Naoki |last5=Ozaki |first5=Kiyoaki |date=2020 |title=Migration of black-naped terns in contrasted cyclonic conditions |url=https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00227-020-03691-0 |journal=Marine Biology |language=en |volume=167 |issue=6 |page=83 |doi=10.1007/s00227-020-03691-0 |bibcode=2020MarBi.167...83T |issn=1432-1793|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

== Behaviour ==
[[File:Black-naped Tern06.ogv|thumb|Lady Elliot Island, Qld, Australia]]

=== Hunting and diet ===
Like most terns, this species hunts by diving; they partially breach the surface of the water and directly capture their prey carrying it horizontally in their beak. Most hunt alone at sea, but hunt closer to shore in the surf when feeding nestlings and mates.<ref name=":0" />

==== Diet ====
The diet consists of small fish up to 10 cm long, including those in families:<ref name=":0" />

* [[Old World silverside|Atherinidae]] (hardyheads and sprats);
* [[Anchovy|Engraulidae]] (anchovies);
* [[Halfbeak|Hemiramphidae]] (garfish);
* [[Flying fish|Exocoetidae]] (flying fish);
* [[Mullet (fish)|Mugilidae]] (mullet);
* [[Barracuda|Sphyroenidae]] (barracudas);
* [[Carangidae]] (trevally or jacks);
* and [[Scombridae]] (tuna).

=== Reproduction and development ===

==== Breeding grounds ====
The black-naped tern breeds on oceanic islands in small colonies of 5–20 pairs, occasionally up to 200. Nests are made past [[Tide|high tide]] in depressions in sand or rock, sometimes delineated with shells or [[coral]] fragments, otherwise with little preparation. The rocky outcrops may be returned to yearly during the breeding season.<ref name=":3">{{Cite journal |last1=Hulsman |first1=Kees |last2=Smith |first2=Geoffrey |date=1988 |title=Biology and Growth of the Black-naped Tern Sterna Sumatrana: An Hypothesis to Explain the Relative Growth Rates of Inshore, Offshore and Pelagic Feeders |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1071/MU9880234 |journal=Emu – Austral Ornithology |language=en |volume=88 |issue=4 |pages=234–242 |doi=10.1071/MU9880234 |bibcode=1988EmuAO..88..234H |issn=0158-4197|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

==== Laying period ====
Laying time varies from May to December depending on the location of the colony. Egg laying begins in [[India]] and [[Polynesia]] in May, followed by colonies in the [[Philippines]] in July, north-east [[Australia]] in September, oceanic Indian colonies from September to November, and the [[Great Barrier Reef]] from November to December. Leading up to egg laying, the male feeds the female her daily requirement of fish.<ref name=":3" />

==== Clutch size and brooding ====
[[File:Sterna sumatrana MHNT.ZOO.2010.11.131.15.jpg|thumb| ''Sterna sumatrana'' eggs, [[MHNT]]]]
Clutch size varies from island to island, between 1 and 3 eggs per pair. Both mates take shifts incubating the eggs for 21–23 days; shifts last anywhere between 4 minutes and 7 hours. Once the chicks hatch, the mates alternate in feeding and brooding for the first 7 days. As the chicks reach later stages of development, the mates hunt simultaneously.<ref name=":3" />

==== Predation ====
Until the chicks reach a weight of roughly 65 g, they are vulnerable to predation by gulls; at this point, they are too large for the gulls to carry. Development to this weight takes 10.5 days; since the parents protect their nest for the first 7 days, chicks spend 3.5 days more vulnerable to predation. To defend against this, parents camouflage the nest by removing eggshell remnants and defecating several meters away to avoid bright eye-catching material near the chicks. When aerial predators are present, chicks hide themselves among vegetation and debris.<ref name=":3" />

On one occasion, a colony of black-naped terns were observed using [[Mobbing (animal behavior)|mobbing]], shrill cries, and defecation as defence mechanisms against an encroaching [[grey heron]] during the breeding season.<ref name=":2" />

==== Fledging ====
Chicks fledge after 21–23 days; however, fledglings rely on their parents for at least 2 months afterwards. Parents and fledglings therefore leave breeding grounds together.<ref name=":3" />

=== Calls ===
This tern calls with short, high-pitched, repeated sharp notes; "chit", "chip", "chrrut", "tsip".<ref name=":1" />

zh.extract:
黑枕燕鸥（学名：Sterna sumatrana）又名苍燕鸥，为鸥科燕鸥属的鸟类，海洋性燕鷗，主要分佈於太平洋和印度洋的熱帶和亞熱帶地區，很少見於內陸地區。该物种的模式产地在蘇門答臘島。


描述
這種燕鷗長約30厘米，翼長21-23厘米。牠們的喙和腿是黑色的，但喙尖是黃色的。蒼燕鷗有長而分叉的尾羽。它們的臉和胸部是白色的，背部和翅膀呈灰白色。其最初的幾根初級飛羽是灰色的。


亚种
有兩個已列出的亞種：

S. s. mathewsi （Stresemann, 1914） – 印度洋西部的島嶼
黑枕燕鸥指名亚种 S. s. sumatrana （Raffles, 1822） – 從印度洋東部島嶼到太平洋西部和澳大拉西亞。在中国大陆，分布于河北、山东、浙江、福建、广东、海南等地。该物种的模式产地在苏门答腊。


参考文献

2007 IUCN Red List of Threatened Species（页面存档备份，存于互联网档案馆）
BirdLife International 2004. Sterna sumatrana. In: IUCN 2007. 2007 IUCN Red List of Threatened Species.
